coredata.dc:title: An effective and simple strategy for highly selective and anti-interference detection of dopamine based on the carbon quantum dots-molecularly imprinted polymers modified electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wei
Zhang, Liling
Sun, Shengmiao
Wang, Bixuan
Jiang, Ling
Zhu, Liying
coredata.dc:description: Dopamine (DA) dysfunction is known to cause neurological and physiological disorders, such as schizophrenia, Parkinson's disease, and addictive behavior. Therefore, dopamine detection is essential for both diagnosis and disease prevention. In this study, a novel and effective electrochemical sensor was fabricated based on a carbon quantum dots (CQDs)@molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) modified electrode for dopamine detection. The CQDs were obtained by an eco-friendly hydrothermal carbonization method, using sycamore leaves as a natural carbon source. The structure of the prepared CQDs@MIPs composite was confirmed through techniques including Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering technique, scanning electron microscopy, and transmission electron microscopy. By combining the rapid electron transfer rate of the CQDs with the selective activity of the MIPs, the prepared CQDs@MIPs composite-modified electrode demonstrated greatly enhanced electrochemical performance. The developed sensor exhibited a good response towards dopamine detection, with a considerably lower detection limit (5 × 10−9 M) and a wider linear range (5 × 10−9–5 × 10−4 M) compared to current publications. In addition to the outstanding reproducibility and anti-interference ability, this ‘green sourced’ sensor showed great potential for practical applications, as validated by the satisfactory recovery analysis of dopamine from goat blood serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Dopamine
Electrochemical sensor
Natural carbon source

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
Effect of CQDs@MIPs concentration for DA detection
Effect of incubation time for DA detection
Electrochemical DA detection of CQDs@MIPs/SPCE under optimized conditions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis spectrum of CQDs, with digital photographs of water and CQDs under UV light (left: water, right: CQDs solution) on the corner; (B) TEM image of CQDs; (C) DLS spectrum of CQDs.
(A) SEM image of CQDs@MIPs; (B)(C)(D) TEM image of CQDs@MIPs.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms and (B) Electrochemical impedance spectra of bare SPCE (pink), NIPs/SPCE (blue), CQDs@NIPs/SPCE (red), CQDs@MIPs/SPCE (black). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Effect of CQDs@MIPs concentration on the performance of electrochemical sensor, Conditions: experimental group: 0.1 μM of DA at pH 7.5, control group: PBS at pH 7.5, different amounts of CQDs@MIPs; (B) effect of incubation time on the performance of electrochemical sensor, Conditions: experimental group: 0.1 μM of DA at pH 7.5, control group: PBS at pH 7.5, 2.0 mg/mL of CQDs@MIPs.

(A) Differential pulse voltammetry graphs of electrochemical sensor with different concentration of DA; (B) The current response-DA concentration calibration curve. Conditions: 1.0 mg/mL of CQDs@MIPs, 20 min of incubation time.
Evaluation of the performance of the electrochemical sensor: (A) Reproducibility and (B) Anti-interference.
Synthesis of CQDs@MIPs composite and modification of electrochemical sensors.
The comparison of electrochemical sensor performance between the CQDs@MIPs/SPCE electrode with previously reported electrode for DA detection.
Percent recovery of DA detection in goat blood serum samples.

head.ce:title: An effective and simple strategy for highly selective and anti-interference detection of dopamine based on the carbon quantum dots-molecularly imprinted polymers modified electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Light Industry, State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Light Industry
State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Light Industry, State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dopamine (DA) dysfunction is known to cause neurological and physiological disorders, such as schizophrenia, Parkinson's disease, and addictive behavior. Therefore, dopamine detection is essential for both diagnosis and disease prevention. In this study, a novel and effective electrochemical sensor was fabricated based on a carbon quantum dots (CQDs)@molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) modified electrode for dopamine detection. The CQDs were obtained by an eco-friendly hydrothermal carbonization method, using sycamore leaves as a natural carbon source. The structure of the prepared CQDs@MIPs composite was confirmed through techniques including Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering technique, scanning electron microscopy, and transmission electron microscopy. By combining the rapid electron transfer rate of the CQDs with the selective activity of the MIPs, the prepared CQDs@MIPs composite-modified electrode demonstrated greatly enhanced electrochemical performance. The developed sensor exhibited a good response towards dopamine detection, with a considerably lower detection limit (5 × 10−9 M) and a wider linear range (5 × 10−9–5 × 10−4 M) compared to current publications. In addition to the outstanding reproducibility and anti-interference ability, this ‘green sourced’ sensor showed great potential for practical applications, as validated by the satisfactory recovery analysis of dopamine from goat blood serum.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a critical catecholamine neurotransmitter in the mammalian brain, serving a vital purpose in the proper functioning of human metabolism, cardiovascular, central nervous, renal and hormonal systems [1–3]. Even a minor change in DA levels could make a big difference in brain behaviors of motivation, cognition, emotion, memory, and reward. Moreover, a lot of research findings have revealed that various neurological and physiological diseases, including schizophrenia, Parkinson's disease, bipolar disorder, attention hyperactivity disorder and addictive behavior, could be contributed to the abnormal secretion levels of DA [4–6]. Therefore, developing a rapid and specific detection method for DA concentration is of great importance for diagnostic and therapeutic applications.

In recent decades, numerous analytical techniques, such as fluorescence spectroscopy (FD) [7], spectrophotometry (SP) [8], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [10], have been proposed and utilized for DA determination. Compared to these DA determination methods, electrochemical sensors offer plenty of advantages which make them especially suitable for technological applications, such as high sensitivity, ease of implementation, superior chemical stability, quick analysis and low costs [11–14]. The surface material of electrodes is one of the most important factors determining the performance of electrochemical sensors; thus, surface modification of electrodes has become a focus of research. There has been an increasing interest in developing nanomaterials for use in electrochemical sensing and biosensors owing to their strong electrical conductivity, large surface energy, and favourable interaction with other molecules [15–18]. Electrochemical sensors with respect to various nanomaterials such as metal–organic frameworks, metal nanoparticles, carbon-based materials, and molecularly imprinted polymers, and their use in DA detection have been extensively investigated, as highlighted in existing literature. For instance, Devi et al. [19] reported an exfoliated carbon quantum dots modified electrode for the detection of DA. Jayasekhar Babu et al. [14] introduced a DA detection sensor using a photoluminescence carbon nano dots-based electrode as a suitable sensor. A DA sensor was reported using Pd-Pt alloy nanoparticles decorated polyoxometalate electrode by Jiao et al. [20]. Furthermore, Yang et al. [21] designed a molecularly imprinted polymer-decorated electrochemical sensor for selective and robust DA detection.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are promising carbon nanomaterials with spherical-like structure and a particle size smaller than 10 nm, exhibiting advantages of good dispersion, good oxidation–reduction quality, and strong electrochemical performance [11,22–24]. CQDs were first explicitly described by Xu et al. [25], and the understanding of CQDs has been accelerating over the last decades. The applications of CQDs in photocatalyst [26], photothermal therapy [27], and biological imaging [28] have been widely recognized. Past methods used for CQDs synthetization include laser ablation [29], hydrothermal synthesis [30], acidic oxidation [31], microwave pyrolysis [32], and arc discharge [33]. Unfortunately, these methods each have their limitations, such as complexity, high cost, extensive acid usage, and massive chemical waste. In recent years, there has been an adept awareness of using natural biomass (such as wood [34], sugarcane bagasse [35], fruit peel [14], and waste tea residue [36]) as a huge source of carbon, and there is increasing research exploring the natural carbon sources and eco-friendly methods for the synthesis of CQDs [14,37]. It is worth noting that, despite the numerous advantages of carbon-based materials, one of the major roadblocks for their use in electrochemical sensors is the weak selectivity and recognition of target molecules [38]. In practice, a major challenge in the electrochemical determination of DA is interference from ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA), which are known for their antioxidant properties and co-exists with DA in biological matrixes at concentrations 100–1000 times higher than DA [3,39]. Furthermore, the overlapping oxidation of DA, UA and AA occurs due to the close oxidation potential of DA, UA and AA at conventional electrodes such as Ag [40], Pt [41], and glassy carbon electrode (GCE) [42]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop effective sensing nanomaterials to enable selective DA determination. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), also termed as artificial antibodies, are synthetic affinity materials with recognition properties for specific target molecules [21,43,45]. MIPs are synthesized by polymerizing monomers in the presence of a template molecule, which serves as a mold for the polymer. Upon template removal, MIPs then display tailor-made binding sites complementary to the template molecules in shape, size, and functionality. In this way, MIPs are capable of cleaning up complex samples with structurally similar compounds, and facilitating the specific isolation of target compounds in a rapid and robust manner [44–45]. MIPs exhibit excellent physical and chemical stability, applicability to a wide range of targets, low costs, favourable engineering properties and an easy integration into standard processes [45]. All these features, combined with their remarkable selectivity and affinity for their target, allowed MIPs to be successfully applied to the selective detection of DA. However, the incomplete removal of templates and weak conductivity of MIPs may limit their application in sensor fields. Many studies have highlighted the synergistic benefits of using nanomaterials in combination, for example, composites materials of carbon nanotubes and nanoparticles or new coating materials. [11,14,19,46–48,60]. By utilizing appropriate techniques to combine diverse types of nanomaterials, it is highly possible to enhance the electrochemical performance of the modified electrodes. Recently, the combination of MIPs with various nanoparticles and quantum dots has led to the development of many new types of nanosensors that exhibit improved selectivity and sensitivity in electrochemical sensing applications [12,21,43,50–51]. These MIPs and their hybrids hold significant promise for widespread use in the detection of food, water samples, biological molecules, and other applications. For example, Yola [49] designed an exceptional selectivity and environmental compatibility molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on CdMoO4/g-C3N4 nanocomposite.

Based on the synergetic effects between CQDs and MIPs, this research presents a simple and effective approach for fabricating a high-performance electrochemical sensor modified with CQDs@MIPs nanocomposite (Scheme 1 ). An oven-derived hydrothermal carbonization method was employed to synthesize the CQDs using sycamore leaves as a natural carbon source. CQDs were further combined with MIPs via a facile hydrothermal route, obtaining a CQDs embedded MIPs (CQDs@MIPs) nanocomposite. After the CQDs@MIPs nanocomposite was deposited on the surface of a screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE), a novel CQDs@MIPs/SPCE electrochemical sensor was constructed for the detection of DA. This sensing method is simple and straightforward, avoiding complex chemical reactions. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report where leaves waste sourced CQDs and MIPs worked together in one screen-printed carbon as one electrochemical sensor. The developed sensor utilizing CQDs@MIPs/SPCE held superior advantages in DA detection, including excellent selectivity, anti-interference, and high reproducibility, showing great potential for determining DA content in practical products.

3-Hydroxytyramine hydrochloride (dopamine), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), 2,2′-Azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN), bisphenol A (BPA), and uric acid (AA) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). L-Lysine, L-Arginine and L-Leucine were commercially supplied by Sigma Aldrich Co., Ltd. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Ascorbic acid, glucose, acetic acid and calcium chloride (CaCl2) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 4-vinylphenylboric acid (4-VPBA) and magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O) were respectively purchased from Ark Pharm, Inc. (Libertyville, IL, USA) and Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Goat blood serum was ordered from Shenggong Biological Engineering Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) was offered by Yi Yue Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). The aqueous solutions involved in the experiments were prepared using ultrapure water. In addition, the chemical regents were of analytical grade.

The dynamic light scattering particle size analyser (DLS; Omni 280171, Brookhaven, Inc., New York, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained with a Lambda 25 Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Inc., Massachusetts, USA). A Jem-2100 Transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) and a Hitachi SU8010 scanning electron microscope (SEM; High Technologies, Tokyo, Japan) were employed to obtain images for studying the structure and surface morphology of the synthesized materials. All electrochemical measurements were performed on the CHI 660E electrochemical work station provided by Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

An oven mediated hydrothermal method was used to prepare CQDs according to the method described by Jayasekhar Babu et al. [14] with modifications. Briefly, air dried sycamore leaves waste biomass was collected from the school campus and then heated using a domestic oven operated at 200 °C for 4 h. The carbonized sycamore leaves were ground into a fine powder. A mixed solution containing the ground powder and 500 mL of ultrapure water was stirred for 24 h, after which the solution was centrifuged under the conditions of 15000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was then collected and freeze dried in a lyophilizer for more than 48 h. The prepared CQDs powder were stored for further use at 4 °C.

The preparation of CQDs@MIPs nanocomposite was derived from previous work reported by Ensafi et al. [12]. Briefly, 0.15 g of CQDs was dispersed in 21 mL of acetonitrile-PBS mixture (pH 7.5) as solution A using ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, and 20 mM of EGDMA and 50 mg of AIBN were added into the above solution. Afterwards, 0.1 g of DA powder and 2.5 mmol of 4-VPBA were dissolved in 30 mL acetonitrile-PBS mixture (pH 7.5) as solution B under anaerobic and light-avoidant conditions at 4 °C for 3 h, mixed with solution A. After oxygen removal by nitrogen purging, the mixed solution was heated at 70 °C for 16 h in an oil bath. The mixture was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and the obtained product was washed 3–4 times with a mixture of methanol/acetic acid (3:1 v/v) to remove the template molecule. Ultimately, CQDs@MIPs nanocomposite was dried in the oven at 60 °C. Non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) and CQDs@NIPs were synthesized under the same conditions as the MIP, but without the addition of DA.

The surface of SPCE was coated with 20 μL of CQDs@MIPs, and subsequently dried at 60 °C. The surface-modified electrode was thoroughly rinsed with ultrapure water at least three times. After drying under a nitrogen atmosphere, the CQDs@MIPs/SPCE electrode was stored at 4 °C for further use. The CQDs@NIPs/SPCE electrode and NIPs/SPCE electrode, which served as the control groups, were prepared following the same procedures as the CQDs@MIPs/SPCE electrode.

In order to minimize the influence of sample matrix, goat blood serum was filtered and ten-fold diluted with the phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.5). Then, the prepared DA standard solution was added to the prepared goat blood serum samples to determine the recovery rate and relative standard deviation of DA.

The formation of CQDs sourced from sycamore leaves was confirmed through both visual observation and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, based on the fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots [12,52]. As seen in Fig. 1 A, CQDs exhibited the emission of blue-green photoluminescence (PL) under UV irradiation at 260–320 nm, and the spectrum absorption peak observed at 280 nm might be attributed to the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC bond [12]. The surface morphology and size of the CQDs were characterized by TEM and DLS. As demonstrated in Fig. 1B and Fig. 1C, a near-spherical shape of CQDs with an average particle size of about 6–8 nm was observed.

Both SEM and TEM images of the CQDs@MIPs nanocomposite were used to study their morphologies and structures, as seen in Fig. 2 . The images revealed that the prepared CQDs@MIPs nanocomposite consisted of a network of nanospheres with approximately 200 nm diameter interconnected with each other (Fig. 2A and 2B). From Fig. 2C and 2D, the appearance of black particles (below 10 nm) indicated that the CQDs@MIPs were synthesized successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behaviors of different modified electrodes (bare SPCE, NIPs/SPCE, CQDs@NIPs/SPCE, CQDs@MIPs/SPCE) were characterized in a solution of [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- containing 0.1 M KCl by using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As seen in Fig. 3 A, the CV profiles of various electrodes exhibited clearly defined redox peaks during both forward and reverse scans. Surprisingly, it was found that the bare electrode showed a more favourable current response as compared to the electrode modified with NIPs. This implies that the conductivity of the polymers used in NIPs were too poor to foster better electron transfers in the redox probe compared to the bare electrode. Cai et al. [53] have reported very similar results, where the bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) provided better anodic current than the graphene oxide modified GCE in [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- redox probe. However, the modification of CQDs@NIPs nanocomposite on the electrode resulted in an elevation in the oxidation current response. In addition to the good electro-conductivity of CQDs, the enhanced oxidation current response observed in the nanocomposite modified electrode could be attributed to the strong synergistic effect of CQDs and NIPs in the nanocomposite. This effect is believed to increase the effective surface area and transfer rate of electrons [12,31], thus leading to improved electrochemical performance. The best electrochemical performance was obtained with the CQDs@MIPs modified electrode. Compared to CQDs@NIPs nanocomposite, imprinting cavities were formed within the MIPs after elution, which further magnified the surface properties of the electrode and accelerated the electron transfer between the redox probe and the CQDs@MIPs modified electrode. This result is in agreement with findings of Ensafi et al. [12], where the electrons in the conduction bands of the CQDs@MIPs composite could transfer to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of the template molecules (promethazine hydrochloride).

The electrochemical impedance spectra of each electrode investigated using a 5.0 mM solution of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4−/0.1 M KCl were illustrated in Fig. 3B, which is usually used to interpret the kinetic mechanism of electron transfer between the electrode and the electrolyte. Normally, the EIS spectra are composed of two regions: the high-frequency semicircular region, which reflects kinetically controlled processes, and the low-frequency linear region, which corresponds to diffusion-controlled processes. The surface resistance of the sensor is directly proportional to the diameter of the semicircle. Compared to bare SPCE and NIPs/SPCE, an obviously smaller semicircle observed at CQDs@NIPs/SPCE electrode indicated the excellent electrocatalytic activity of CQDs. Also, Fig. 3B clearly showed that the smallest semicircle diameter was offered by CQDs@MIPs/SPCE electrode. The low impedance of CQDs@MIPs composite confirmed the effective removal of dopamine template and the formation of template cavities that allow [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- to reach the electrode surface, resulting in a decrease in resistance value. These EIS results are in agreement with the findings from the CV characterization. Overall, the experimental results from both CV and EIS suggested that the electro-conductivity and surface properties of MIPs can be greatly improved by properly introducing CQDs nanoparticles in to MIPs. Herein, the CQDs@MIPs composite was selected as a suitable electrode material for further research to the determination of DA in this research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs@MIPs concentration for DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of CQDs@MIPs shows vital impacts on the performance of the electrode: a low concentration of CQDs@MIPs covered on the electrode surface might result in suboptimal electrode performance, while a high film thickness formed by a high concentration of CQDs@MIPs might adversely affect electron transfer in the redox probe. Based on this theory, the effect of CQDs@MIPs with different concentration (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, and 2.5 mg/mL) on the electrochemical detection performance of modified electrodes was recorded in the presence of 0.1 μM of DA in PBS at a pH of 7.5, as shown in Fig. 4 A. The CQDs@MIPs/SPCE electrode exhibited the highest peak current of 91.48 µA at a concentration of 1.0 mg/mL, which was selected as the optimal CQDs@MIPs concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of incubation time for DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: Since the adsorption of DA on the surface of CQDs@MIPs is time-dependent, the incubation time of electrode to DA was further optimized. Fig. 4B presents the current response of CQDs@MIPs/SPCE electrode for different times incubated in DA samples (0.1 μM). The oxidation peak current significantly decreased rapidly (from 64.91 to 58.23 μA) with increasing incubation time and then generally levelled off after a 20 min incubation. Therefore, in order to obtain a better electrocatalytic response, at least a 20 min incubation was required for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical DA detection of CQDs@MIPs/SPCE under optimized conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized experimental conditions, the analytical ability of the CQDs@MIPs/SPCE electrode on the detection of DA with different concentrations was carried out by using DPV, a higher sensitive and accurate voltammetry technique. The DPV current response of the CQDs@MIPs/SPCE modified electrode for different levels of DA in 0.1 M PBS was presented in Fig. 5 A. It was found that the peak current dropped as the DA concentration increased. This result could be attributed to the fact that the massive adsorption of biological molecules on the surface of MIPs lead to a reduction in electron transfer rate. From Fig. 5B, a linear relationship (Ip (µA) = −11.607LgCDA + 118.64) between concentrations of DA and peak current was observed, with a corresponding correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.996. Meanwhile, the CQDs@MIPs/SPCE modified electrode showed a linear detection range from 5 × 10−9–5 × 10−4 M with a considerably low detection limit of 5 × 10−9 M, as estimated by the signal to noise ratio of 3 (S/N = 3). Table 1 presented and compared the observed electrochemical performance of the CQDs@MIPs/SPCE modified electrode with other published DA electrodes, showing the better electrochemical performance of CQDs@MIPs/SPCE electrode in the detection of DA. The greatly reduced detection limit and enhanced detection range of CQDs@MIPs/SPCE electrode proved again its high electron transfer rate and good catalytic properties. Moreover, the high surface area of SPCE provides strong interaction with CQDs@MIPs composite. It can be therefore concluded that the CQDs@MIPs/SPCE modified electrode is an excellent electrode for DA detection.

Under the same analysis conditions, five aforementioned CQDs@MIPs/SPCE electrodes were prepared for repetitive measurement studies. The relative standard deviation (R.S.D) of the five repetitive DA (0.1 mM in PBS) detection performance was found to be 3.53%, see Fig. 6 A, indicating the excellent reproducibility of the CQDs@MIPs/SPCE modified sensor. In addition, there is a risk that a variety of possible interfering molecules present in real biological fluids will corrupt the detection of DA by using electrochemical sensor. To thoroughly determine the anti-interference ability of CQDs@MIPs/SPCE modified electrode, a broader range of interferential species (up to twelve interferential species, including UA, AA, glucose, CaCl2, Bisphenol A, KCl, MgCl2, L-Lysine, L-Arginine, and L-Leucine, Phenylephrine, Noradrenaline) were introduced to examine the selective detection of DA as compared to previously reported electrodes [11,20,41]. As presented in Fig. 6B, the interference intensity for DA detection after the addition of interfering molecules was 3.19% with respect to the DPV response of the sensor to DA (100%), which was almost negligible. These results suggest an excellent selectivity of CQDs@MIPs/SPCE modified sensor for detecting the target molecule.

In order to evaluate the performance of the proposed CQDs@MIPs for quantifying DA in actual samples like goat blood serum, a recovery method was employed. All collected goat blood serum were diluted with PBS solutions (pH 7.5) without any other pretreatment before analysis. These goat blood serum samples were then spiked with varying amounts of DA, and the DA levels were measured using the standard addition method. The percent recoveries were calculated following the procedure reported by Elugoke et al. [11], obtaining average recoveries ranging between 96.4% and 102.3% (Table 2 ) with the RSD less than 2.48%. In the enzyme-linked aptamer assay for the determination of DA in serum as reported by Paeng et al. [61], the recovery percentage of serum DA was approximately 95%. These results indicated that our proposed electrode detection method is sufficiently applicable for the DA detection in practical samples.

This research reports the fabrication of a ‘green sourced’ CQDs@MIPs/SPCE modified electrode which is firstly applied for the DA detection. The synthetic method of CQDs demonstrated here is chemical free, cost-effective and eco-friendly. These synthesized CQDs, sourced from leaves waste, exhibit a spherical shape with an average particle size of about 6–8 nm, possessing large surface area and high electro-conductivity. The MIPs were prepared by creating molecular locks to match template molecules. The existence of imprinting cavities supplies abundant anchor sites to selectively adsorb DA molecules on the CQDs@MIPs surface. These unique properties of MIPs and CQDs combined together make the CQDs@MIPs nanocomposite an ideal surface material for improving the electrochemical performance of electrode, enabling electrochemical sensor to detect target molecules with high selectivity, anti-interference, and precision. The obtained results revealed that the electrochemical sensor had a fairly lower detection limit and a broadly linear range. Moreover, the aforementioned electrode has been successfully used for recovery test in goat blood serum, indicating the CQDs@MIPs/SPCE is a promising system for analysis of DA in practical applications.

Wei Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Liling Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Visualization. Shengmiao Sun: Data curation. Bixuan Wang: Data curation. Ling Jiang: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Liying Zhu: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Key R&D Program of China (2021YFC2102700), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2106228, 32060004).

View

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective and instantaneous upconversion fluorescent nanoprobe for ascorbic acid detection in biological samples
Chin. Chem. Lett.

Tang, A highly selective and instantaneous upconversion fluorescent nanoprobe for ascorbic acid detection in biological samples, Chin. Chem. Lett. 28 (2017) 1983-1986, http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cclet.2017.08.024.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step preparation of carbon quantum dots-reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite–modified glass carbon electrode for the simultaneous detection of ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid
Ionics

Fang, One-step preparation of carbon quantum dots-reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite–modified glass carbon electrode for the simultaneous detection of ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid, Ionics 26 (2020) 5817-5828, https://doi.org/10.1007/s11581-020-03703-5.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Morphology–dependent electrochemical sensing properties of iron oxide–graphene oxide nanohybrids for dopamine and uric acid
